kept.text:
🇫🇷 🇮🇹 Thales Alenia Space a complètement revu ses designs de satellites et son processus d’industrialisation pour s’adapter aux exigences du NewSpace, avec une production pouvant atteindre 2 mini-satellites par semaine.  

📡 L’observation de la Terre représente un tiers du chiffre d’affaires de l’entreprise et mobilise près de 2 000 salariés, grâce à des technologies duales civiles et militaires de très haut niveau.  

🇮🇩 L’entreprise combine optique et radar dans une offre « ALL-IN-ONE » permettant un suivi H24, indépendamment des conditions météo ou de la nuit, avec un premier contrat déjà signé en Indonésie.  

🌍 Grâce à ses expertises française en optique/infrarouge et italienne en radar, TAS atteint des performances exceptionnelles, comme visualiser l’ombre d’un câble de télésiège depuis 500 km d’altitude.  

⚡ Face aux tensions géopolitiques, l’entreprise passe d’une logique de haute résolution à des constellations à haute revisite, tout en restant compétitive face aux acteurs du NewSpace américain.

🤝 Thales Alenia Space appartient à :Thales Group (67 %) et Leonardo (33 %).

https://t.co/q04ZldG1XA

kept.text:
The South African private sector, Multi-National Corporations, financial institutions and law firms have been implicated in corruption, white collar crime and also as enablers of corruption,money laundering since 1994 starting with the Arms Deal.

It would be seriously mistaken to think that the private sector and Multi-National Corporations   are the savior of RSA from a corrupt public sector. 

Since 1994, the South African private sector—including Multi-National Corporations (MNCs), banks, auditors, and law firms—has transitioned from being perceived as "unwitting participants" to being identified by investigative bodies as active "enablers" and "facilitators" of systemic corruption. 

The Historical Roots: The 1999 Arms Deal
The 1999 Strategic Defence Procurement Package (Arms Deal) established a blueprint for private sector collusion with the state. 

MNC Involvement: French arms giant Thales (formerly Thomson-CSF) and its South African subsidiary Thint were implicated in paying bribes to high-ranking officials, including former President Jacob Zuma, to secure contracts and protect against investigations.

Enabling Networks: The deal saw the rise of "politically-connected businessmen" and consultants who used front companies to funnel kickbacks, earning hundreds of millions in sub-contracts.

Banking & Financial Scrutiny: Investigations by the Scorpions later highlighted how these payments were moved through financial advisory networks, though many findings were initially suppressed or "sanitised". 

State Capture & Global Professional Services
The Zondo Commission of Inquiry (2018–2022) exposed how reputable global firms provided the "infrastructural support" necessary for state capture. 

Consulting & Auditing:
Bain & Company was found to have colluded with the presidency to restructure the South African Revenue Service (SARS), effectively "capturing" and weakening the tax agency.

McKinsey & Company and KPMG were implicated in facilitating contracts at state-owned enterprises (SOEs) like Transnet and Eskom.

PwC faced criticism for failing to detect multi-billion Rand fraud while auditing South African Airways (SAA).

The Banking Sector:
Nedbank was highlighted for "disturbing" involvement in interest-rate swap deals at Airports Company South Africa (ACSA).

Major banks including Standard Bank, ABSA, and Sasfin have faced allegations of failing to flag—or even actively facilitating—gold-smuggling and money laundering networks.

Legal "Gatekeepers":
Law firms have been classified as "high risk" for money laundering, frequently using legal professional privilege to shield the movement of illicit funds and the creation of shell companies. 

Current Status & Global Impact
FATF Greylisting: In early 2023, South Africa was placed on the Financial Action Task Force (FATF) "grey list" due to systemic weaknesses in its anti-money laundering (AML) and counter-terrorist financing (CFT) controls, particularly in the private sector.

Economic Cost: Corruption and white-collar crime are estimated to drain approximately R147 billion annually from the South African economy through illicit financial flows.

Legal Reforms: Recent legislative shifts, including the National Prosecuting Authority Amendment Bill (2023), aim to establish permanent anti-corruption units to more aggressively prosecute corporate enablers.
